title: Fixing Type Inference for Type Aliases

statement.markdown:
Type inference is a powerful feature of TypeScript. However, it can sometimes be tricky to work with. In this question, you will be given a function that uses a type alias to type-check an argument. However, the function contains a logical bug. Your task is to fix the bug and make the function work correctly.

Here is an example of how the function should work:

```typescript
function processArray(arr: number[]): number[] { return arr; }
console.log(processArray([1, 2, 3])); // Output: [1, 2, 3]
```
statement.inputFormat: A string representing an array of numbers
statement.notes: The function should return the input array unchanged.

constraints.description: The size of the input array

examples.explanation: The function should return the input array unchanged.
The function should return an empty array.

hints.text: Pay attention to the type alias definition

editorial.title: Editorial
editorial.intuition: The bug is caused by the way the type alias is defined. To fix it, we need to add a type parameter to the type alias.
editorial.optimalSolution:
Here is the optimal solution:
```typescript
function processArray<T>(arr: T[]): T[] { return arr; }
console.log(processArray([1, 2, 3])); // Output: [1, 2, 3]
```
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The corrected function works as expected.
commonMistakes: Not adding a type parameter to the type alias
